text: The surgeon utilizes the Lone Star retractor to provide enhanced exposure for deeper tracts during the operation, allowing for better visualization and access to the surgical site.
The surgeon uses a microcautery device, specifically a Colorado needle, to perform precise cauterization during the anal surgery procedure.
The surgeon carefully removes granulation tissue up to the external sphincter, ensuring the area is clear of any remaining tissue.
The surgeon displays the cored out fistula, which has been removed in one piece, showing the clean excision from the surrounding tissue.
The surgeon temporarily packs the external wound with gauze to ensure hemostasis, controlling any visible bleeding at the site.
The surgeon removes the temporary packing from the external wound before proceeding to suture the internal opening.
The surgeon performs a large elliptical excision to remove all separated tissue, ensuring a healthy tip of the flap and creating a suitable landing zone for adequate closure.
A detailed view of the excised internal opening is shown, highlighting the precise removal of tissue.
The surgeon displays the de-epithelialized landing zone, which provides adequate coverage for the closed internal opening.
The surgeon carefully closes the defect in the intra-sphincteric space, originating from the internal opening, using resorbable braided sutures.
The video shows a large defect in the tissue following adequate debridement, highlighting the extent of tissue removal necessary for the procedure.
The surgeon uses electrocautery to create a surgical flap, a technique commonly employed in anal surgery.
The surgeon creates a flap measuring four to five centimeters in length, ensuring tension-free coverage below the sutured internal opening.
The surgeon sutures the flap in place, utilizing one of several possible techniques to secure it over the surgical site.
The surgeon uses two barbed sutures to incrementally fixate the flap, starting from the proximal end and moving distally.
The surgeon ensures the flap is securely fixed and visually assesses its perfusion, indicating healthy blood flow within the tissue.
